title: Iran Targets Subsea Cables, China-EU Race for AI Compute Sovereignty
subhead: Iran's plan to control Hormuz internet cables signals a new digital chokepoint, while Canada and China advance major AI infrastructure projects. The US-China summit yields rare earth concessions, and Poland warns of escalating infrastructure cyberattacks.

sections.heading: Iran Moves to Control Hormuz Subsea Internet Cables
sections.body:
Iranian state-linked media announced a plan to impose fees and licensing requirements on undersea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz, marking a significant escalation in the country's efforts to leverage its geographic position. Military spokesperson Ebrahim Zolfaghari declared on X that Iran will "impose fees on internet cables," while IRGC-linked media indicated that companies such as Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Amazon would be required to comply with Iranian law and pay licensing charges.

The proposal would reserve cable repair and maintenance rights for Iranian firms. While it remains unclear how Iran could enforce compliance given US sanctions that bar tech giants from making payments to Tehran, experts warn the threat alone carries weight. IRGC-affiliated figures have signaled that the Revolutionary Guards could target cables through combat divers, small submarines, and underwater drones.

The cables carry over 95% of international data traffic between Europe, Asia, and the Persian Gulf. A disruption would affect banking systems, military communications, AI cloud infrastructure, and financial trading—potentially triggering what researchers call a "cascading digital catastrophe across several continents."
sections.whyItMatters: Iran is expanding its Hormuz leverage beyond oil to include the digital arteries of global internet infrastructure, creating a new category of chokepoint vulnerability that affects cloud computing, financial systems, and military communications.

sections.heading: Canada Builds 320 MW 'Sovereign AI' Gigafactory
sections.body:
HIVE Digital Technologies announced a CAD $3.5 billion AI infrastructure project in the Greater Toronto Area, designed to host more than 100,000 GPUs at full build-out with 320 megawatts of utility capacity. The facility, targeting operation in the second half of 2027, would become one of Canada's largest AI gigafactories.

The project emphasizes "sovereign AI infrastructure"—the vision that countries must own their compute capacity rather than rent from foreign data centers. "Countries that own the machines will write the future," said Frank Holmes, Executive Chairman of HIVE and its BUZZ HPC subsidiary. "Countries that rent compute from data centers abroad will not contain sensitive data within their borders."

The facility positions itself within the Toronto-Waterloo corridor, between the University of Toronto's Vector Institute and Waterloo's engineering ecosystem. HIVE now has over 850 MW of power globally, including 450 MW of operating data centers, with a pipeline targeting approximately 130,000 GPUs.
sections.whyItMatters: Nations are racing to build domestic AI compute capacity as sovereignty concerns mount over data residency, national security, and the economic value of AI workloads.

sections.heading: China Launches 18 Satellites for 10,000-Bird Constellation
sections.body:
China deployed 18 communications satellites on Sunday from the Hainan International Commercial Aerospace Launch Center, advancing the Spacesail Constellation (formerly G60) toward its goal of more than 10,000 satellites in low-Earth orbit by the end of 2030. The launch brought the constellation to 162 satellites in orbit.

The Long March 8 rocket placed the satellites in preset orbital positions. Spacesail, operated by Shanghai-based Innovation Academy for Microsatellites, aims to provide high-speed, secure broadband internet services globally—positioning China as a direct competitor to Starlink.

The launch marked China's 33rd space mission this year and the 643rd flight of the Long March rocket fleet. The rapid cadence underscores Beijing's commitment to building sovereign space-based communications infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: China is accelerating construction of a state-controlled alternative to Western satellite internet constellations, with implications for global connectivity sovereignty and information flows.

sections.heading: US-China Summit Yields Rare Earth Concessions
sections.body:
Following the Trump-Xi summit in Beijing—the first US presidential visit to China since 2017—the White House announced that China will address US concerns regarding rare earth supply chain shortages, including yttrium, scandium, neodymium, and indium. China also agreed to lift prohibitions on the sale of rare earth production and processing equipment and technologies.

The agreement is part of a broader package including China's commitment to purchase at least $17 billion per year of US agricultural products through 2028, approval for 200 Boeing aircraft, and restoration of US beef market access. The two countries established new U.S.-China Boards of Trade and Investment to manage bilateral economic relations.

Both leaders agreed that "Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon" and called for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, stating that "no country or organization can be allowed to charge tolls." China controls approximately 60% of global rare earth production and over 85% of processing capacity.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit produced tactical trade relief on critical minerals while leaving underlying tech competition unresolved. China's willingness to ease rare earth restrictions suggests both sides seek stability, but the concession does not alter the structural dependency of Western supply chains.

sections.heading: Poland Warns of Infrastructure-Focused Cyberattacks
sections.body:
Poland's Internal Security Agency (ABW) published a report documenting a sharp escalation in nation-state cyberattacks targeting industrial control systems and critical infrastructure through 2024 and 2025. State-backed actors targeted military facilities, water treatment plants, electricity systems, and transportation networks—shifting from espionage and data theft toward physical disruption.

The agency recorded over 40,000 incident reports and a 5.5 million alerts, with an 18% year-over-year increase in attacks. Key techniques included QR phishing ("quishing"), social engineering campaigns, and exploitation of cloud infrastructure for malware delivery.

The report highlighted a security breach at multiple Polish water treatment plants that enabled state-sponsored threat actors to manipulate industrial control systems. Poland's proximity to the Ukraine conflict has made it a frontline target for Russian and Belarusian cyber operations.
sections.whyItMatters: The Polish experience shows how nation-state cyber operations are transitioning from data exfiltration to physical infrastructure disruption—a model that could spread to other regional conflicts.

sections.heading: AI Cybersecurity Foundation Cracks Under New Threats
sections.body:
A Council on Foreign Relations analysis warns that three long-standing cybersecurity assumptions are no longer valid in the AI era: that sophisticated attacks remain expensive, that identity systems built for humans can extend to AI agents, and that human judgment stays in the decision loop.

CrowdStrike documented an 89% year-over-year increase in AI-enabled adversary operations, with China-nexus intrusions rising 38% in 2025—a tempo impractical without AI assistance. In November 2025, Anthropic disclosed that Chinese state-sponsored actors had used its Claude model to run a largely automated cyberespionage campaign, with AI performing 80-90% of the work across roughly 30 targets.

The report calls for an "audit of assumptions"—identifying where threat models still assume capability is scarce, where identity systems assume a human operator, and where informal human judgment has been automated away without replacement controls.
sections.whyItMatters: The security assumptions underlying AI deployment are outdated before the infrastructure is even built. Defenders must close all paths while attackers need only one, and patching cycles built for human tempo cannot match machine-speed vulnerability discovery.

sections.heading: Satellite Constellations Called 'Unregulated Geoengineering'
sections.body:
Scientists from University College London warn that the rapid growth of satellite mega-constellations constitutes an "unregulated geoengineering experiment" without ethical forethought. Professor Eloise Marais told NPR that black carbon (soot) particles from rocket launches and satellite reentries remain in the upper atmosphere for 2.5-3 years—far longer than surface pollution—creating a cooling effect similar to proposed solar geoengineering schemes.

While the current impact is small compared to ground-level pollution, the space industry's projected growth could quickly magnify the effect. The particles could potentially deplete ozone in higher atmospheric layers and alter circulation patterns.

SpaceX plans to launch millions of satellites through its Starlink and other constellation programs. The researchers note there is no solid regulatory framework governing responsible and sustainable use of orbital space.
sections.whyItMatters: The rush to deploy satellite constellations for communications and data services is creating unintended atmospheric consequences that no international framework currently addresses.

bottomLine: Iran's move to control subsea internet cables represents a new form of digital leverage that complements its oil chokepoint strategy, while the US-China summit produced tactical relief on rare earths without resolving structural tech competition. Canada's 320 MW AI gigafactory and China's Spacesail constellation launch show both sides building sovereign compute and communications infrastructure. Poland's warning about infrastructure-focused cyberattacks and CFR's analysis of AI security assumptions highlight how threat models are shifting faster than defensive frameworks. The satellite mega-constellation buildout is accumulating unintended atmospheric consequences that lack any regulatory oversight.
sources: Reuters, CNN, Council on Foreign Relations, Poland ABW, HIVE Digital Technologies, China Daily, NPR, Ars Technica, White House Fact Sheet, InterAksyon/PhilStar, ProPakistani, Moneycontrol, IAPP, Mining Weekly, The Whig/Newsfile
